items.q: What tense follows 'depuis' for ongoing situations in French?
items.correct: Present tense (J'habite ici DEPUIS 5 ans - I have lived here for 5 years).
items.misconception: Past tense (because it is translated as 'have lived' in English).
items.why: Students translate the English tense literally. French uses PRESENT with depuis because the action is ongoing - 'I am still living'. The English perfect tense misleads students into using past in French.

items.q: What is the correct form of 'parler' (to speak) for 'nous'?
items.why: Students apply the same ending across persons. ER verbs use different endings: je parle, tu parles, il parle, NOUS parlons, vous parlez, ils parlent. Each subject takes its own ending.

items.q: What follows 'si' (if) in a conditional sentence like 'If I were rich, I would travel'?
items.correct: Imperfect tense (Si j'ÉTAIS riche, je voyagerais).
items.why: A classic error - students put conditional after si. The rule: si + IMPERFECT in the if-clause, conditional in the result clause. Never 'si je serais' - always 'si j'étais'.
It's Septante. “Soixante-dix” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Femme. “Mere” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Number 90 in Belgian/Swiss French (nonante) versus France's quatre-vingt-dix?
items.why: Exact answer: 90. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
